quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to electron affinity as you move from left to right across a period?

options.text: It generally becomes more negative (more energy released)
It generally becomes less negative (less energy released)
It stays exactly the same across the entire period
It alternates between positive and negative values

quiz_questions.explanation: Electron affinity generally becomes more negative across a period, meaning atoms release more energy when gaining an electron. This is because the increasing nuclear charge attracts additional electrons more strongly.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which group of elements generally has the most negative electron affinity (releases the most energy when gaining electrons)?

options.text: Group 1, alkali metals
Group 18, noble gases
Group 17, halogens
Group 2, alkaline earth metals

quiz_questions.explanation: Halogens (Group 17) have the most negative electron affinities because they need only one more electron to complete their octet, and gaining that electron releases a large amount of energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to electron affinity as you move down a group?

options.text: It generally becomes more negative down the group
It stays constant throughout the entire group column
It generally becomes less negative down the group
It increases dramatically with each new period added

quiz_questions.explanation: Electron affinity generally becomes less negative going down a group because larger atoms with more electron shielding are less effective at attracting an additional electron.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following elements has the most negative electron affinity?

options.text: Sodium
Chlorine
Calcium
Argon

quiz_questions.explanation: Chlorine has the most negative electron affinity of all elements. It strongly attracts an additional electron because it needs just one more to complete its octet.

quiz_questions.question_text: Do noble gases have favorable electron affinities?

options.text: Yes, they strongly attract extra electrons to their shells
No, their full outer shells resist gaining extra electrons
Yes, but only the heavier noble gases like xenon and radon
No, because noble gases do not have any electrons at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Noble gases have unfavorable (positive or near-zero) electron affinities because their outer shells are already full. Adding an electron would go into a new, higher energy level, which is not energetically favorable.

quiz_questions.question_text: What sign is the electron affinity value when energy is released as an atom gains an electron?

options.text: Positive, indicating energy is absorbed
Negative, indicating energy is released
Zero, indicating no energy is exchanged
It has no sign and is always unsigned

quiz_questions.explanation: When energy is released as an atom gains an electron, the electron affinity is negative. A more negative value means the atom more strongly attracts electrons.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do Group 2 elements (alkaline earth metals) have nearly zero or positive electron affinities?

options.text: They already have a full s sublevel which is relatively stable
They have too many protons to attract any electrons at all
They have no electrons in their outer shell to begin with
They are metals and metals always have positive electron affinity

quiz_questions.explanation: Group 2 elements have a full s sublevel (2 electrons in s orbital), which is relatively stable. Adding another electron would require starting a new, higher-energy p sublevel, which is not favorable.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is electron affinity the same thing as electronegativity?

options.text: Yes, they are different names for the exact same property
No, electron affinity is for isolated atoms while electronegativity is within bonds
Yes, but electron affinity only applies to metals while electronegativity applies to nonmetals
No, electron affinity measures losing electrons while electronegativity measures gaining them

quiz_questions.explanation: They are related but different. Electron affinity measures the energy change when an isolated gaseous atom gains an electron. Electronegativity measures how strongly an atom attracts shared electrons within a chemical bond.

quiz_questions.question_text: If an element has a very negative electron affinity, would it likely be a metal or nonmetal?

options.text: A metal, because metals gain electrons easily
A nonmetal, because nonmetals tend to gain electrons
A metalloid, because they have intermediate properties
A noble gas, because they have full outer shells already

quiz_questions.explanation: Elements with very negative electron affinities are typically nonmetals, especially halogens. They strongly attract electrons to complete their outer shells.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does electron affinity help predict what type of ion an element will form?

options.text: Elements with very negative electron affinity tend to form negative ions
Elements with very negative electron affinity tend to form positive ions
Electron affinity has no relation to what type of ion an element forms
All elements form the same type of ion regardless of electron affinity

quiz_questions.explanation: Elements with very negative electron affinities readily gain electrons, so they tend to form negative ions (anions). Halogens, with the most negative electron affinities, commonly form -1 ions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is electron affinity?

options.text: The energy change when a gaseous atom gains an electron from its surroundings
The energy needed to remove an electron from a neutral gaseous atom completely
The tendency of an atom to attract shared electrons in a chemical bond formed
The distance from the nucleus to the outermost electron in a neutral atom today

quiz_questions.explanation: Electron affinity is the energy change that occurs when a neutral gaseous atom gains an electron. A negative value means energy is released, indicating the atom readily accepts electrons.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'exothermic' mean when discussing electron affinity?

options.text: A process that releases energy to the surroundings as heat or light
A process that absorbs energy from the surroundings during the reaction
A process that occurs only at extremely high temperatures in a laboratory
A process that does not involve any exchange of energy with surroundings

quiz_questions.explanation: An exothermic process releases energy. When an atom with negative electron affinity gains an electron, energy is released because the atom becomes more stable.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'endothermic' mean when discussing electron affinity?

options.text: A process that requires energy input and absorbs energy from surroundings
A process that releases energy and heat to the surrounding environment
A process that only happens inside living organisms and biological cells
A process that results in no change in energy for the atoms involved

quiz_questions.explanation: An endothermic process requires energy input. Atoms with positive electron affinity (like noble gases) require energy to force them to accept an additional electron.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'negative electron affinity value' mean?

options.text: The atom releases energy when it gains an electron becoming more stable
The atom loses energy levels when an electron is added to its outer shell
The atom has fewer electrons than protons in its neutral atomic state today
The atom cannot gain any electrons under any conditions or circumstances

quiz_questions.explanation: A negative electron affinity value means the process of gaining an electron is exothermic -- energy is released. The more negative the value, the more strongly the atom attracts electrons.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an anion?

options.text: A negatively charged ion formed when an atom gains one or more electrons
A positively charged ion formed when an atom loses one or more electrons
A neutral atom that has equal numbers of protons and electrons in it
A particle found inside the nucleus alongside the protons and neutrons

quiz_questions.explanation: An anion is a negatively charged ion created when a neutral atom gains one or more electrons. Elements with high electron affinity readily form anions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'electron configuration stability' mean?

options.text: How energetically favorable an atom's electron arrangement is overall
The physical strength of the bonds holding electrons in their orbitals
The speed at which electrons move around the nucleus of a single atom
The number of electrons that can fit in the first energy level of any atom

quiz_questions.explanation: Electron configuration stability refers to how energetically favorable an atom's arrangement of electrons is. Full shells, half-full subshells, and completed octets are especially stable configurations.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'kilojoules per mole' mean as a unit for electron affinity?

options.text: The energy in thousands of joules exchanged per mole of atoms gaining electrons
The number of moles of electrons that can be added to one kilogram of atoms
The mass of electrons in kilograms that one mole of atoms contains in total
The temperature in degrees at which one mole of atoms begins gaining electrons

quiz_questions.explanation: Kilojoules per mole (kJ/mol) measures the energy change in thousands of joules when one mole of gaseous atoms each gain one electron.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'electron gain' mean?

options.text: The process of an atom accepting an additional electron into its structure
The process of an atom releasing an electron from its outermost shell
The increase in size of an electron as it moves to a higher energy level
The movement of an electron between two bonded atoms in a molecule made

quiz_questions.explanation: Electron gain is the process of a neutral atom accepting an additional electron, which changes it into a negatively charged anion. Electron affinity measures the energy associated with this process.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a halide ion?

options.text: A halogen atom that has gained one electron to form a -1 charged ion
A halogen atom that has lost one electron to form a +1 charged ion
A halogen atom in its neutral state before any reaction has occurred
Two halogen atoms bonded together forming a diatomic molecule pair

quiz_questions.explanation: A halide ion is formed when a halogen gains one electron, giving it a -1 charge and a full outer shell. Examples include fluoride (F-), chloride (Cl-), and bromide (Br-).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'periodic trend in electron affinity' mean?

options.text: The predictable pattern of electron affinity values across periods and down groups
The random changes in electron affinity that cannot be predicted by position
The constant electron affinity value shared by all elements in the same period
The change in electron affinity caused by changing temperature in a laboratory

quiz_questions.explanation: The periodic trend in electron affinity describes the predictable pattern: electron affinity generally becomes more negative across a period and less negative down a group.

quiz_questions.question_text: Chlorine has an electron affinity of -349 kJ/mol and sodium has -53 kJ/mol. Which element more readily gains an electron?

options.text: Sodium, because its electron affinity value is closer to zero overall
Chlorine, because its more negative value means more energy is released
Both gain electrons equally well since they are in the same period row
Neither gains electrons because both values are negative numbers here

quiz_questions.explanation: Chlorine more readily gains an electron. Its electron affinity of -349 kJ/mol means much more energy is released when it gains an electron compared to sodium's -53 kJ/mol.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that nitrogen has a less negative electron affinity than carbon, even though nitrogen is further right in the period. Why does this exception occur?

options.text: Nitrogen has a stable half-filled p sublevel that resists gaining another electron
Nitrogen has fewer protons than carbon which makes it attract electrons less well
Carbon is actually to the right of nitrogen on the periodic table not to the left
The trend always goes in the opposite direction for nonmetals in the second period

quiz_questions.explanation: Nitrogen has a half-filled p sublevel (one electron in each of the three p orbitals), which is especially stable. Adding an electron would require pairing, which is energetically less favorable.

quiz_questions.question_text: Neon has a positive electron affinity, meaning energy must be added to force it to accept an electron. Why?

options.text: Neon already has a completely full outer shell so adding an electron is unfavorable
Neon has no electrons at all so there is nothing to attract another electron to it
Neon is too small for any additional electrons to physically fit around its nucleus
Neon is a metal and all metals resist gaining electrons due to their electron structure

quiz_questions.explanation: Neon's outer shell is completely full with 8 electrons. An extra electron would have to go into a new, higher energy level far from the nucleus, which requires energy input rather than releasing it.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist is designing a battery and needs an element that strongly attracts electrons. Based on electron affinity trends, which area of the periodic table should they look?

options.text: Bottom left, where the alkali metals are located on the periodic table
Upper right, where the halogens and reactive nonmetals are on the table
Far right column, where the noble gases are located on the periodic table
Center of the table, where the transition metals are located in the table

quiz_questions.explanation: Elements in the upper right of the periodic table (excluding noble gases) have the most negative electron affinities. Halogens like fluorine and chlorine are the strongest electron attractors.

quiz_questions.question_text: Fluorine has a less negative electron affinity than chlorine (-328 vs -349 kJ/mol), even though fluorine is higher in Group 17. Why?

options.text: Fluorine is so small that its electrons repel an incoming electron in the tiny space
Fluorine has fewer protons than chlorine so it cannot attract electrons as strongly
Chlorine is a gas at room temperature while fluorine is a solid making it different
The measurement for fluorine is incorrect and should actually be more negative here

quiz_questions.explanation: Fluorine is so small that its existing 7 electrons are packed tightly together, creating strong electron-electron repulsion. This makes it slightly harder for an incoming electron to join, despite fluorine's high nuclear charge.

quiz_questions.question_text: Lithium has an electron affinity of -60 kJ/mol. Would lithium be more likely to gain or lose an electron in chemical reactions?

options.text: Gain an electron, because its electron affinity is negative in value
Lose an electron, because its ionization energy is much lower than its electron affinity
Neither, because lithium is completely unreactive like a noble gas element
Both equally, because metals can gain and lose electrons at the same rates

quiz_questions.explanation: Although lithium has a slightly negative electron affinity, it much more readily loses its single valence electron (low ionization energy). Metals like lithium form positive ions, not negative ones.

quiz_questions.question_text: An element has a very negative electron affinity and high electronegativity. It forms a -1 ion easily. Which group is it most likely in?

options.text: Group 1, the alkali metals family
Group 2, the alkaline earth metals
Group 17, the halogens family
Group 18, the noble gases family

quiz_questions.explanation: Very negative electron affinity, high electronegativity, and forming -1 ions are all characteristic of halogens (Group 17). They need one electron to complete their octet.

quiz_questions.question_text: Beryllium has a positive electron affinity. A student predicts magnesium (below beryllium in Group 2) also has a positive or near-zero electron affinity. Is this a good prediction?

options.text: No, magnesium should have very negative electron affinity because it is larger
Yes, Group 2 elements have full s sublevels making electron gain unfavorable
No, elements lower in a group always have more negative electron affinity
Yes, but only because magnesium has the same number of valence electrons

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a good prediction. All Group 2 elements have full s sublevels, making them relatively resistant to gaining additional electrons. Their electron affinities are positive or near zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: Oxygen has an electron affinity of -141 kJ/mol for gaining its first electron. However, its second electron affinity is positive (+744 kJ/mol). Why is gaining the second electron so unfavorable?

options.text: Adding a second electron to an already negative ion requires overcoming repulsion
Oxygen can only hold one extra electron in its outer shell before it becomes full
The second electron goes into the nucleus instead of the electron cloud around it
Oxygen changes into a different element after gaining its first extra electron here

quiz_questions.explanation: After gaining one electron, oxygen becomes O-. Adding a second electron means forcing a negative electron onto an already negatively charged ion, which requires significant energy to overcome the repulsion.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student compares the electron affinities of elements across Period 3. They notice the general trend but see that sodium's value (-53 kJ/mol) is less negative than expected. What might explain this?

options.text: Sodium would need to start filling a new sublevel which is not as favorable for it
Sodium has only one valence electron and gaining one does not complete a stable shell
Sodium is actually a noble gas and noble gases do not have favorable electron affinity
The sodium value is incorrect and should be recalculated with better lab equipment used

quiz_questions.explanation: Sodium has 1 valence electron. Gaining one more would give it only 2 valence electrons, which does not achieve a stable octet. Since gaining an electron does not greatly increase stability, the energy released is small.
